claims.statement: MFi auth is exercised twice per wireless pairing (carplay-20260521-101016): once at the iAP2 layer over Bluetooth RFCOMM and once at the AirPlay layer over WiFi/RTSP.
MFi iAP2-layer flow over Bluetooth RFCOMM: 0xAA00 ReqAuthCert -> 0xAA01 945-byte cert -> 0xAA02 ReqChallenge (20-byte challenge) -> chip RSA-signs -> 0xAA03 128-byte signature -> 0xAA05 AuthSuccess.

`0xAA00 ReqAuthCert` → `0xAA01` 945-byte cert →
   `0xAA02 ReqChallenge` (20-byte challenge) → chip RSA-signs → `0xAA03` 128-byte signature →
   `0xAA05 AuthSuccess`.

claims.statement: MFi AirPlay-layer flow over WiFi/RTSP: after /pair-verify the firmware does a second chip signature (log 'MFi auth create signature') producing another 128-byte signature; no separate /fp-setup or /auth-setup RTSP endpoint exists; the AirPlay-side MFi material rides inside the HomeKit pairing exchange; there is no FairPlay layer on this firmware.

claims.statement: The ARMAndroidAuto segfault is not a user-visible defect: the ARMadb-driver supervisor's _hu_link_main auto-restarts within ~400ms, no core dump is left behind, and the next plug cycle works normally.

claims.statement: The segfault tail-log is a useful marker for 'last clean session end'; if absent and instead Host No Response or watchdog traces appear, the disconnect cause is different (USB timeout, heartbeat loss).

claims.statement: The crash site (waitForUSBDevice inside ARMAndroidAuto) suggests a double-wait or null-accessory race when USB detach happens while the AA daemon is mid-descriptor-probe.

claims.statement: Apple-side wireless-CarPlay authentication is performed by two separate mechanisms: (1) the hardware MFi 2.0C coprocessor on I²C bus 1 (/dev/i2c-1:0x11) driving the iAP2 0xAA00-0xAA05 certificate + RSA-1024 challenge-response, and (2) HomeKit-style pair-setup/pair-verify on the AirPlay channel.
claims.quote:
(1) the **hardware MFi 2.0C coprocessor** on I²C bus 1 (`/dev/i2c-1:0x11`), which
drives the iAP2 `0xAA00`–`0xAA05` certificate + RSA-1024 challenge-response, and (2)
**HomeKit-style pair-setup / pair-verify** on the AirPlay channel.

claims.statement: 9 iAP2 engines run in parallel with AirPlay, parsing structured metadata: Identify, MediaPlayer, CallState, Communication, RouteGuidance, Location, Power, VehicelStat, WiFiConfig.

claims.statement: MFi function chain: APSMFiPlatform_CopyCertificate (cert read), APSMFiPlatform_CreateSignature (sign) → mfi_auth_processchallenge (low-level register sequence) → _DoI2C (I²C transfers against /dev/i2c-1).

MFi register map: 0x30/0x31 = AccessoryCertLen/AccessoryCertData (945 bytes), 0x20/0x21 = ChallengeDataLen/ChallengeData (20 bytes), 0x10 = AuthControlStatus (trigger/status), 0x11/0x12 = SignatureLen/SignatureData (128-byte RSA-1024).
MFi iAP2 message flow: 0xAA00 ReqAuthCert → 0xAA01 Cert (945 B) → 0xAA02 ReqChallenge (20 B) → 0xAA03 ChallengeResponse (128 B) → 0xAA05 AuthSuccess.
claims.quote:
0xAA00 ReqAuthCert  →  0xAA01 Cert (945 B)  →  0xAA02 ReqChallenge (20 B)
   →  0xAA03 ChallengeResponse (128 B)  →  0xAA05 AuthSuccess

claims.statement: The MFi coprocessor is exercised twice per wireless CarPlay pairing: once for the iAP2-over-Bluetooth challenge-response, and once for the AirPlay-over-WiFi 'MFi auth create signature' step (after pair-setup/pair-verify).
claims.quote: The coprocessor is exercised **twice per wireless CarPlay pairing**

claims.statement: Capability detection functions: CheckMultiAudioBusCap (multi-bus audio support), CheckMultiAudioBusVersion (audio bus version), CheckMultiAudioBusPolicy (audio routing policy), IsSupportBGRecord ('Hey Siri'/'OK Google' support), IsUsePhoneMic (external/app microphone check).

claims.statement: Conversion capabilities: sample rate 8↔16↔44.1↔48 kHz, channels mono↔stereo, bit depth 16-bit PCM primary, buffer push/pop with conversion ratios.

What the firmware does: protocol translation (IAP2/NCM USB ↔ CPC200-CCPA protocol), basic AAC decoding (compressed→PCM), format conversion (sample rate, channels, bit depth), hardware configuration (codec init, mixer settings), audio routing (stream classification/direction), buffer management (simple I/O buffering).

The firmware DOES perform WebRTC AGC (Automatic Gain Control, applied to microphone audio), WebRTC AECM (Acoustic Echo Cancellation Mobile, 8kHz/16kHz only), and WebRTC NS (Noise Suppression, configurable via VoiceQuality setting).

The firmware does NOT do automotive-specific optimizations, complex multi-stream mixing, advanced real-time DSP beyond WebRTC, or multi-channel processing beyond basic routing (host app responsibility).
claims.quote: ### What Firmware Does NOT Do (Host App Responsibility):

claims.statement: iPhone encoder boundaries: bitrate is an autonomous adaptive algorithm (750K floor, 20% burst budget) and VideoBitRate hint may be ignored; frame drops are zero (encoder processes every submitted frame, all drops occur downstream); frame rate is variable 13-27 fps content-dependent with CustomFrameRate as ceiling not target; ForceKeyFrame is the only external control point (triggers full encoder restart, host sends via Command FRAME 0x0C); resolution is set during AirPlay negotiation (host specifies in Open message).
claims.quote: | Frame drops | **Zero** — encoder processes every submitted frame | All drops occur downstream |

claims.statement: Pixel encoder boundaries: bitrate 4.03 Mbps VBR fixed at config time (adapter maxVideoBitRate acts as cap not target); frame drops zero during uninterrupted streaming (all 6 runs); frame rate ~29.2fps (PowerBasedLimiter enforced 30fps ceiling, declines to 6.5-13.8fps when idle); ForceKeyFrame PROHIBITED (causes full Pixel UI reset, not just encoder restart); IDR interval 60s (sync-frame-interval=60000000 μs, measured 62-68s); resolution 1280x720 Gearhead configured (GhostActivityDisplay→encoder surface).
claims.quote: | ForceKeyFrame | **PROHIBITED** — causes full Pixel UI reset | NOT just encoder restart like CarPlay |

claims.statement: Bluetooth link key storage directory structure /tmp/bluetooth/[LOCAL_ADDR]/ (e.g. DE:AD:BE:EF:00:05) contains files: linkkeys, names, features, manufacturers, lastused, classes, config, and services (at root level), verified via adapter TTY log Jan 2026.

Link key file formats: linkkeys (paired device keys), names (cached friendly names by MAC), features (LMP feature bitmask per device), manufacturers (manufacturer ID per device), lastused (Unix timestamp of last connection), classes (CoD Class of Device per device), config (adapter configuration), all Text format.
Link key format is 'MAC_ADDRESS LINK_KEY_HEX KEY_TYPE PIN_TYPE', example: DE:AD:BE:EF:00:07 68851F93529776F17B9A155512568EA5 5 -1.

Link key type values: 0=Combination key, 1=Local unit key, 2=Remote unit key, 3=Debug combination key, 4=Unauthenticated P-192, 5=Authenticated P-192, 6=Changed combination key, 7=Unauthenticated P-256, 8=Authenticated P-256.
Bluetooth config file format includes 'class 0x000408', 'onmode discoverable', 'mode off'.

DeletedDevList is a JSON array tracking devices scheduled for removal from pairing, e.g. [{"id": "DE:AD:BE:EF:00:07", "name": ""}] with id=Bluetooth MAC address and name=device name (may be empty), verified via adapter TTY log Jan 2026.
claims.quote: The `DeletedDevList` is a JSON array tracking devices scheduled for removal from pairing

claims.statement: DeletedDevList is referenced by RiddleBluetoothService_Interface_Control during device removal operations and persisted in configuration to handle removals across reboots.
claims.quote: Referenced by `RiddleBluetoothService_Interface_Control` during device removal operations. Persisted in configuration to handle removals across reboots.
